title: Rolled sugar cookies with royal icing
subtitle: Crisp, buttery cut-out biscuits decorated with a firm royal icing glaze.
excerpt: Rolled sugar cookies are firm, buttery biscuits cut into shapes and decorated with royal icing. The dough is straightforward and rolls cleanly without cracking. The royal icing sets to a hard, smooth surface that can be coloured and piped. This is the standard template for decorated biscuits at Christmas, Easter, and celebrations generally.
sourceNotes: Rolled and cut biscuits decorated with icing have a long history in European baking. German Lebkuchen, British shortbread, and American sugar cookies all share the same basic structure: flour, fat, sugar, egg, and a flavouring agent. The royal icing decoration tradition in Britain is associated with celebration cakes and biscuits at Christmas and Easter. The format of stamping shapes from chilled dough and decorating with piped royal icing became popular in British home baking from the 1990s through American influences.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the uniced biscuits layered between baking paper for up to two months. Defrost at room temperature before icing.
recipe.batchNotes: The dough doubles well. Roll in batches and keep unused dough in the fridge while each batch bakes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The unbaked dough keeps in the fridge for three days, wrapped. Iced biscuits keep in a tin for up to a week.

recipeTools.notes: Two baking trays lined with baking paper.

glossaryTerms.definition: A stiff icing made from egg whites, icing sugar, and lemon juice. Sets completely hard. Used for decorating biscuits, Christmas cakes, and gingerbread houses. Thin it with a few drops of water for flooding; keep it stiff for piping lines and details.

content.text: Sugar cookies need chilling before cutting and again before baking. A cold dough cuts cleanly and holds its shape in the oven. If the dough becomes warm while you work, return it to the fridge for 10 minutes.
is applied in two ways: a thinner flood coat to fill the shape, and a thicker piped coat for detail. Both use the same base; just adjust the consistency with small amounts of water.

items.prepNote: from large eggs, at room temperature

content.text: Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy, about 3 minutes. Add {{eggs}} one at a time, beating between each. Add {{vanilla-extract}}.
Sift in {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Mix until a smooth dough forms. Divide in half, flatten each half into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for at least 1 hour.
Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Line two trays with baking paper.
Roll one disc of dough on a lightly floured surface to 4 to 5 mm thick. Cut into shapes and place on the lined trays. Re-roll the trimmings and cut again.
Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until the edges are just starting to colour. The centres will look pale and underdone; they firm up as they cool. Transfer to a rack and cool completely before icing.
Make the royal icing and decorate
Whisk {{egg-whites}} until frothy. Add {{icing-sugar}} and {{lemon-juice}} and beat until the icing is thick, white, and holds a stiff peak. Cover with cling film when not using; it sets quickly.

To flood a biscuit: thin a portion of the icing with a few drops of water until it flows slowly off a spoon. Use a small spoon to spread it within the edges of the biscuit. Leave to set for at least 1 hour before adding piped details on top.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Dough handling and icing consistency are the two things to watch.

items.symptom: Cookies spreading and losing their shape.
items.fix: Return the dough to the fridge for 10 minutes whenever it becomes sticky. Warm dough spreads; cold dough holds its outline.

items.symptom: Royal icing cracking after it sets.
items.cause: Icing too thick when applied, or dried too fast.
items.fix: Thin the flood icing a little more. Dry in a cool, stable environment rather than near a heat source.
